flashcards.Back: A Cisco-proprietary Layer 2 protocol used to discover and share information about directly connected neighboring Cisco devices, including device ID, software version, and interface details.
An industry-standard, vendor-neutral Layer 2 protocol (802.1AB) that performs similar discovery functions to CDP but works across devices from different manufacturers.
A local database on a network device that stores information gathered via CDP or LLDP, essential for mapping the physical network topology and troubleshooting connectivity.
A technology that bundles multiple physical Ethernet links between two switches into a single logical link, providing higher bandwidth and automatic link-level redundancy.
The logical virtual interface created when multiple physical ports are aggregated into an EtherChannel. Configuration applied to this interface affects all bundled physical ports.
The industry-standard protocol (802.3ad) used to negotiate the formation of an EtherChannel. it uses modes 'active' and 'passive' for negotiation.
A Cisco-proprietary protocol for negotiating EtherChannels. it uses modes 'desirable' and 'auto' and is the legacy alternative to LACP.
The interface configuration command used to assign a physical port to an EtherChannel bundle and specify the negotiation protocol/mode.
The process of distributing traffic across the multiple physical links of an aggregated bundle, typically using a hash of source and/or destination MAC or IP addresses.
A Layer 2 protocol (802.1D) designed to prevent loops in redundant Ethernet networks by logically blocking specific paths to ensure only one active loop-free path exists.
The central reference point in an STP topology. it is elected based on the lowest Bridge ID (BID) and all other switches calculate the shortest path toward it.
An 8-byte value used by STP to elect the root bridge, consisting of a 2-byte Bridge Priority and the 6-byte MAC address of the switch.
A 12-bit portion of the Bridge Priority field used in PVST+ to carry the VLAN ID, allowing a switch to have a unique BID for each VLAN instance.
The single port on a non-root switch that provides the lowest cumulative path cost to reach the root bridge.
The port on a network segment (collision domain) that is responsible for forwarding traffic from that segment toward the root bridge.
An STP port state where the interface does not forward data frames or learn MAC addresses. it only processes BPDUs to detect network changes.
The operational STP state where a port processes BPDUs, learns MAC addresses, and forwards data frames normally.
An intermediate STP state where a port processes BPDUs to build the network topology but does not yet learn MAC addresses or forward data.
An intermediate STP state where a port populates its MAC address table based on incoming frames but still does not forward data.
The specific management frames used by STP to exchange topology information, elect the root bridge, and maintain loop-free paths.
Cisco's default spanning tree mode (based on 802.1w) that provides significantly faster convergence than legacy STP while maintaining a separate instance for each VLAN.
A Cisco feature that allows an access port to immediately skip the listening and learning states and enter forwarding, typically used for ports connected to end-user devices.
A security feature that automatically disables a PortFast-enabled port if it receives a BPDU, preventing loops from unauthorized switches connected at the edge.
A configurable value (0–61440 in increments of 4096) used to influence the root bridge election. Lower values increase the chance of becoming the root bridge.
A value assigned to an interface based on its bandwidth (e.g., 10 Gbps = 2, 1 Gbps = 4). STP uses this to calculate the shortest path to the root bridge.
The interval (default 2 seconds) at which the root bridge sends out configuration BPDUs to its neighbors.
The duration (default 20 seconds) a switch waits after missing a BPDU before concluding that a topology change has occurred.
The time (default 15 seconds) a port spends in each of the listening and learning states before transitioning to the forwarding state.
A port in Rapid STP that has an alternative path to the root bridge but is currently blocked. it can transition to a root port almost instantly if the primary fails.
A port in Rapid STP that provides a redundant path to the same network segment as another designated port on the same switch. it remains blocked unless the primary designated port fails.
